skill.name: code-review-excellence
skill.description: Master effective code review practices to provide constructive feedback, catch bugs early, and foster knowledge sharing while maintaining team morale. Use when reviewing pull requests, establishing code review standards, mentoring developers, and conducting security audits.
skill.summary: Transform code reviews from gatekeeping to knowledge sharing through constructive feedback, systematic analysis, and collaborative improvement.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All 65 static findings are false positives. The skill is a code review education and guidance resource. Detected patterns (eval mentions, cryptographic references, backtick syntax) are all documentation and educational examples within the playbook, not executable malicious code.

high_findings.title: Documentation Reference to eval()
high_findings.description: Line 405 contains a security checklist item '- [ ] No eval() or similar dynamic execution?' - this is teaching reviewers what to look for in code, not actual eval usage.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: This is a checklist item in documentation, not executable code. The skill teaches code reviewers what security issues to look for.

high_findings.title: Cryptographic Algorithm References
high_findings.description: Lines 25, 66, 82, 92, 174, 337-339, 374-375 contain references to weak cryptographic algorithms in security checklists - these are educational items teaching reviewers what to check for.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: These are security checklist items like 'Passwords hashed (bcrypt/argon2)?' - educational content, not actual cryptographic implementations.

medium_findings.title: Backtick Syntax in Code Examples
medium_findings.description: 45 locations with backtick syntax detected - these are markdown code block delimiters in documentation examples, not shell command execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Backticks are used in markdown to format inline code examples throughout the educational playbook. No actual shell commands are executed.

low_findings.title: Fetch API Examples
low_findings.description: Lines 297 and 304 contain fetch() calls in example code snippets - these are educational examples showing proper async error handling.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: These are TypeScript code examples teaching proper async/await patterns. Documentation examples, not actual network calls.

content.value_statement: Transform code reviews from gatekeeping to knowledge sharing. This skill provides systematic analysis frameworks, practical checklists, and collaborative feedback techniques to catch bugs early while maintaining team morale.
actual_capabilities: Perform systematic code review using proven methodology (context gathering, high-level review, line-by-line analysis, summary)
Identify security vulnerabilities including SQL injection, XSS, and input validation issues
Provide constructive feedback using collaborative language that maintains team relationships
Apply severity labeling (blocking, important, nit, suggestion) to prioritize review findings
Review code across multiple languages including Python, TypeScript, and JavaScript patterns
Conduct architectural and test quality reviews with appropriate depth for PR scope
limitations: Cannot execute code or run tests - only analyzes code statically
Does not have access to project-specific context, coding standards, or team conventions
Cannot access external tooling, CI/CD systems, or repository history
Relies on user to provide complete code context and requirements

use_cases.title: PR Review Enhancement
use_cases.description: Use structured review methodology to provide thorough, consistent feedback on pull requests that catches bugs and educates the author.
use_cases.target_user: Software developers and team leads reviewing code changes

use_cases.title: Onboarding New Reviewers
use_cases.description: Train junior developers on effective review techniques using checklists, severity frameworks, and collaborative language patterns.
use_cases.target_user: Tech leads and mentors mentoring new reviewers

use_cases.title: Security-Focused Audits
use_cases.description: Apply security checklist patterns to identify common vulnerabilities in authentication, input validation, and data protection.

prompt_templates.title: Basic PR Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the following code changes for correctness, security, and maintainability. Provide feedback grouped by severity (blocking issues, important suggestions, minor nits). Include a summary of what was done well.
prompt_templates.scenario: General code review request with code diff

prompt_templates.title: Security-Focused Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Conduct a security review of this code. Check for: input validation, SQL injection risks, XSS vulnerabilities, authentication issues, and sensitive data exposure. Use [blocking] for must-fix issues.
prompt_templates.scenario: When security is a primary concern

prompt_templates.title: Mentorship Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this code with an educational focus. Provide constructive feedback that helps the author learn. Suggest improvements with explanations of why they matter. Use collaborative language.

prompt_templates.title: Quick Sanity Check
prompt_templates.prompt: Give me a quick review of these changes. Focus on the most critical issues (1-3 blocking items maximum). Keep feedback concise.
prompt_templates.scenario: When time is limited or changes are small

output_examples.input: A function that processes user input and saves to database
output_examples.output:
## Summary
Reviewed 3 files, 150 lines changed. Good test coverage.

## Critical Issues
🔴 [blocking] SQL injection risk at line 42 - use parameterized queries

## Suggestions
💡 Consider extracting validation logic to a separate function
🟢 Nice work on comprehensive error handling

## Verdict
✅ Approve after addressing SQL injection

output_examples.input: Python function with mutable default argument
output_examples.output:
## Code Review Findings

### 🔴 [blocking] Mutable Default Argument
Line 15: `def add_item(item, items=[]):` - default list is shared across calls

**Fix:** Use `def add_item(item, items=None):` and initialize inside function.

### 🟡 [important] Missing Type Hints
Consider adding type hints for better maintainability.

### 🟢 [praise] Good docstring and error handling

best_practices: Review within 24 hours to keep PR cycle time low and maintain team momentum
Use severity labels consistently: [blocking] for must-fix, [important] for should-fix, [nit] for nice-to-have
Automate style and formatting checks with linters to focus human review on logic and design
anti_patterns: Blocking PRs for minor style preferences instead of using automated formatters
Using judgmental language like 'This is wrong' instead of collaborative questions
Rubber-stamping approvals without actual review to avoid conflict

faq.question: How long should a code review take?
faq.answer: Aim for 20-30 minutes per review. Break reviews into 60-minute max sessions with breaks. For PRs over 400 lines, request splitting into smaller changes.

faq.question: What if I disagree with the author's response?
faq.answer: Seek to understand their reasoning first. Acknowledge valid points they make. Provide data or benchmarks if concerned about performance. Know when to let go if it's not critical.

faq.question: Should I review my own code before requesting review?
faq.answer: Yes. Do a self-review using the checklist in this skill. Check for obvious issues first. This reduces review cycles and shows respect for reviewers' time.

faq.question: How do I handle large PRs I cannot review thoroughly?
faq.answer: Request the author split large PRs into smaller, focused changes. If you must review, do a high-level architecture review first, then do detailed review on follow-up PRs.

faq.question: What is the difference between blocking and non-blocking comments?
faq.answer: Blocking comments must be addressed before merge - these are bugs, security issues, or serious design problems. Non-blocking are suggestions, questions, or nice-to-have improvements.

faq.question: How do I give feedback on code style preferences?
faq.answer: Direct the author to automated linters and formatters for style issues. Do not manually comment on formatting - automate what you can and focus human effort on logic and design.
